## Mahabharata | Part 3-Amba's story & Satyavati's dilemma solved | His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

### time, cycles, power
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zT9kEv50HVE?t=330
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "You can understand how much more powerful Kartavirya Arjuna must have been to dismiss Ravana like that."
**Summary:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa begins with a recap of the previous episode and then delves into a description of the Yugas, focusing on Treta Yuga and the story of Ravana's encounter with Kartavirya Arjuna, highlighting the latter's immense power.

Hare Krishna! Welcome back to the next episode of the Mahabharata. A quick recap of the last episode. In the last episode, we saw how Vichitra Miria, after marrying Ambika and Ambalika, passed away. And now, his mother, Satyavati, is making some arrangements through his step-brother to conceive children in Ambika and Ambalika so that there could be suitable successors to the throne. We also saw that Amba refused to marry Vichitra Miria, although her two sisters married Vichitra Miria. Today, we will look at these two things: why Amba refused and what happened after her refusal to marry Vichitra Miria. And we will also see what were the arrangements. Before we get there, today we want to first take you a long time back in history, a time known as Treta Yuga. The age in which we are all currently living is called Kali Yuga. This Kali Yuga, as per the Vedas, lasts for 432,000 years, out of which, currently we have roughly past almost 5,000 years. The age previous to Kali Yuga was called Dwapara Yuga. And this Dwapara Yuga lasted for twice the time period of Kali Yuga, which is 864,000 years. And the age previous to Dwapara Yuga was Treta Yuga, which lasted three times that of Kali Yuga. So, the duration of Treta Yuga was 12,096,000 years. And the age previous to that was called Sathya Yuga, which was four times the duration of Kali Yuga. And so, that comes to about 1,728,000 years. So, these 4 Yugas put together constitute one Mahayuga. And in Vedic Knowledge it is understood that time goes always in cycles. That means, the Mahayugas keep repeating. And after this Kali Yuga, next again Satya Yuga will come. Then Treta Yuga, Dwapara Yuga, Kali Yuga. Then again Satya Yuga, Treta Yuga, Dwapara Yuga. Like this, the time goes in cycles according to the Vedas. Now, in Treta Yuga, that was the time when Ravana was trying to establish his supremacy over the world. Ravana was on a tour of Digvijaya, trying to conquer all the kings and subjugate them under him. While travelling like this, he came to the banks of the Narmada and he set camp there. And on the banks of the Narmada, a little further was a place known as Mahishmati. Don't worry, today I'm not going to relate the story of Bahubali because in that movie, that was a fictitious story. It was a fictitious place. We are talking about the real Mahishmati, which is today known as Maheshwar in Madhya Pradesh. That place is there even today. And this Mahishmati was being ruled over by a king at that time by name Kartavirya Arjuna. This Kartavirya Arjuna, he had worshipped Dattatreya, an incarnation of the Supreme Lord. And by his benediction, he had got 1000 alms. While Ravana was Dasagriva, he had 10 heads. Kartavirya Arjuna had 1000 alms. This Kartavirya Arjuna, while Ravana was setting camp on the banks of the Narmada, Kartavirya Arjuna came to sport in the waters of the Narmada along with his queens. And while playing in the waters of the Narmada, very playfully with his alms, he stopped the flow of the water of the Narmada. Because of that, what happened? The water flooded into the camp of Ravana. And Ravana was very arrogant. He was very puffed up because of his immense prowess. He was so powerful that even the devatas would tremble in front of him. Naturally, when he saw that his whole camp had got flooded with water, he got angry. And he said, what is this? Why this flooding has happened? And somebody told him that there is this Kartavirya Arjuna and he has stopped the flow of the water of the Narmada because of which our camp has got flooded. Ravana got extremely angry. He said, I will teach this fellow a lesson. And he sought out Kartavirya Arjuna. And when he saw Kartavirya Arjuna there on the Narmada with all his queens, he started insulting him before then. Kartavirya Arjuna could not brook this insult, especially in front of all his queens. So, what did Kartavirya Arjuna do? Nonchalantly, he captured Ravana as if he was nothing. And he dragged him into the Mahishmati fort. And then, after insulting him like this, he said, after all, what is Ravana? He is nothing. He is nobody. He is a persona non grata. He is an inconsequential person. He said, alright, go. Like that, he sent him away. You can understand how much more powerful Kartavirya Arjuna must have been to dismiss Ravana like that.

---
### envy, incarnations, divine power
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zT9kEv50HVE?t=677
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "This was Parashurama, an incarnation of the Supreme Personality of Godhead."
**Summary:** The lecture continues with the story of Kartavirya Arjuna's encounter with Jamadagni Muni and his Surabhi cow, leading to Parashurama's appearance and a discussion on different types of incarnations.

So this Kartavirya Arjuna, while once he was roaming in the forests surrounding Mahishmati, he came across a hermitage of a sage known as Jamadagni. So this Jamadagni sage, he happened to have a Surabhi cow. A Surabhi cow is a celestial cow, which of course nowadays you don't find. It's not very commonly seen. It's a very rare celestial cow, which Jamadagni Muni happened to possess. This Surabhi cow's speciality is such that any desire you go and express before the Surabhi cow, it will immediately fulfill that wish. It was a Kamadhenu, wish-fulfilling cow. When Kartavirya Arjuna saw this, he became very envious. He was himself very puffed up and arrogant because of his prowess. He was so powerful that even Ramana was nothing in front of him. So he thought, I am the king here. So everything that is best in the kingdom belongs to me. And so by force, he grabbed the Surabhi cow and took it away with him. And Jamadagni Muni, being a simple Rishi, there was nothing much he could do to protest. And so he kept quiet. After a short while, Jamadagni Muni's son who had gone to the forest returned and when he heard this news, Jamadagni's son became very angry. He became infuriated. That how can somebody do this to a sage? This king has lost his senses. He needs to be taught a lesson. So, he immediately picked up a bow and a quiver of arrows and a shield to protect himself and his personal weapon called the Parashu. It was an axe. By now, you must have guessed who this is. This was Parashurama, an incarnation of the Supreme Personality of Godhead. There are different types of incarnations. Vishnu himself is an incarnation known as Purusha Avatara. Generally, people think Vishnu is the source of all incarnations. But the reality is, according to the Shastras, Vishnu himself is an incarnation known as Purusha Avatara. And there are three forms of Vishnu: The Maha Vishnu or Karanodaka Sai Vishnu, Garbhodaka Sai Vishnu and Shirodaka Sai Vishnu or the Paramatma. These are the three forms of the Purusha Avatara or the Vishnu. Then, there are Shakti Avesha Avataras, living entities who are empowered to carry out some superhuman activities. Like, for example, Vyasadeva is a Shakti Avesha Avatara. He is the literary incarnation of the Supreme Personality of Godhead. And he compiled the entire Vedas, which is humanly impossible for one person to do. But he did it because he was an incarnation of the Supreme Lord. He was empowered. He was empowered by the Supreme Lord to carry out this task. Similarly, Prithu Maharaj was another Shakti Avesha Avatara. And Narada Muni is another Shakti Avesha Avatara to spread devotional service. So, this is one kind of incarnation. Then, there are Yuga Avataras. For each Yuga, Satya Yuga, Dvapara Yuga, Treta Yuga, Akal Yuga, there is one special Avatara for each of those ages, which is known as Yuga Avatara.

---
### incarnations, divine plan, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zT9kEv50HVE?t=871
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "What kind of incarnation do you think Parasurama is?"
**Summary:** The speaker continues listing different types of incarnations, including Leela Avataras and Manvantara Avataras, and then poses a question to the audience about the type of incarnation Parashurama is.

Then, there are Leela Avataras, incarnations that the Lord takes to exhibit some specific pastime. Like, there is the incarnation of the fish, Matsya Avatara. Or there is Kurma Avatara, the incarnation of the tortoise. These are all Leela Avataras. Then, there are Manvantara Avataras. In the... There are different Manus. And Manu is the name of a post. And different people occupy this post. Generally, the term of one Manu will be approximately seventy-one and a half Mahayugas. So, that's the duration of one Manu. So, like that, there are many, many Manus who come during the duration of the life of Brahma. Therefore, these are called Manvantara Avataras. Like this, there are many, many incarnations. So, one question for all of you: What kind of incarnation do you think Parasurama is? Type your answers into the comments.

### divine power, devotion, battle
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zT9kEv50HVE?t=943
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Such was the prowess of Parasurama that that highly powerful Kartavirya Arjuna, who very easily defeated Ravana, even he was no match for Parasurama."
**Summary:** The lecture narrates the battle between Parashurama and Kartavirya Arjuna, emphasizing Parashurama's incredible power and revealing him to be a Shakti Avesha incarnation.

In the box, in a short while, we will let you, you will come to know what kind of Avatara Parasurama is. So, this Parasurama, he set out on foot with just this one bow, a quiver of arrows, a shield and one axe. And within a short while, he caught up with Kartavirya Arjuna's team. And they were entering the fort of Mahishmati. And when Kartavirya Arjuna saw this brilliant personality coming in the distance, he could make out that this must be Jamadagni's son, Parasurama. And he must be coming to fight with me to take back the Surabhi cow. So, what did Kartavirya Arjuna do? Kartavirya Arjuna sent his army to tackle Parasurama. Parasurama was all alone. And the army in those days used to consist of something known as an Akshauhini or a phalanx, which consists of 21,870 chariots pulled by horses, an equal amount of elephants, then an additional 109,650 infantry, and to top it all, 65,600 cavalry. All these put together constitutes one Akshauhini. Now, imagine how many Akshauhinis Kartavirya Arjuna would have sent to deal with one single person, Parasurama. Maybe quarter Akshauhini could have done the job. Maybe half an Akshauhini. Or, imagine. Is there even one Akshauhini? Kartavirya Arjuna sent 17 Akshauhinis to deal with Parasurama. And what happened? It is described that when Parasurama came across the 17 Akshauhinis of the army, he started fighting with them at the speed of the mind. Now, imagine the speed of the mind. If I say New York now, immediately your mind will transport you to New York. And if I say London, immediately... Immediately, your mind will be in London. That's the speed at which the mind travels. Within a fraction of a second, the mind can travel thousands of kilometers. Parasurama started fighting at the speed of the mind. And left, right and center, there were hands, legs, severed heads, and there was a river of blood flowing. And the Vedas state that after some time, Parasurama started fighting at the speed of the wind. Now, speed of the wind is lower than the speed of the mind. But still, the speed of the wind is also no less. Why would Parasurama switch over to a lower speed? Because after fighting for some time at the speed of the mind, Parasurama felt a little tired. So, he reduced his speed to the speed of the wind, which is, again, no less. And within a short time, he completely routed the army of Kartavirya Arjuna. And then Kartavirya Arjuna personally had to come out. And he came with 500 bows and simultaneously releasing 500 arrows at Parasurama. Parasurama had only one bow and he could fit only one arrow on it at a time. But still, the speed of Parasurama was such that in no time, he cut off all the bows of, all the 500 bows of Kartavirya Arjuna. And he cut off all the hands of Kartavirya Arjuna also. And then, very quickly, he vanquished Kartavirya Arjuna. Such was the prowess of Parasurama that that highly powerful Kartavirya Arjuna, who very easily defeated Ravana, even he was no match for Parasurama. Why? Because Parasurama was no ordinary person. He was a Shakti Avesha incarnation of the Supreme Personality of Godhead. So, those of you who got that answer right, give yourself a pat on the back.

### vows, duty, determination
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zT9kEv50HVE?t=1176
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Especially in those days, the guru-disciple relationship was so strong, the disciple could never disobey the command of the guru."
**Summary:** The lecture shifts back to Hastinapura and the story of Amba's desire to marry Bhishma, his refusal due to his vow of Brahmacharya, and her subsequent appeal to Parashurama, Bhishma's guru.

Now, we pause this here and we will come back to Hastinapura. In Hastinapura, what had happened, while Ambika and Ambalika married Vichitravirya, Amba had thought, when she was being brought to Hastinapura, Bhishma is going to marry us. And so, she had given her heart to him. And when she was offered to marry Vichitravirya, she refused. And she said, I want to marry Bhishma Dev. But Bhishma Dev, as we all have seen, had taken the vow of Brahmacharya. He told her, my dear lady, I am sorry, it is not possible for me to marry you. Actually, according to the Kshatriya codes, if any woman were to approach a Kshatriya and request him to marry her, he cannot refuse. But Parasurama was bound by his vow of Brahmacharya. And therefore, he could not follow this principle. It was not required for him to follow. When Bhishma Dev explained this, Amba was not in a mood to listen. She was adamant. Bhishma Dev was also not going to budge because he was also firmly determined. Now, Amba is also Kshatriya princess. She is also no less determined. So, she thought, somehow or the other, I want to marry Bhishma Dev. What to do? Now, Bhishma Dev is not listening. So, she thought, let me go to the guru of Bhishma Dev, who taught Bhishma Dev the art of warfare. If guru tells, disciple has to follow. Especially in those days, the guru-disciple relationship was so strong, the disciple could never disobey the command of the guru. So, she approached the guru of Bhishma Dev and explained her position to him and said that she had given her heart to Bhishma Dev, but Bhishma Dev was refusing to marry her. This guru, taking pity, poor girl, young girl, she has somehow, she has given her heart to Bhishma Dev, let me help her. He came to Hastinapura and he spoke to Bhishma Dev and he told him, why don't you marry Amba because it is as per the codes of Kshatriya principles. Bhishma Dev replied, oh my guru Dev, your wish is my command, but then I am bound by my vow of Brahmacharya and so therefore I cannot marry her, kindly excuse me. Then this guru, he responded to Bhishma Dev, oh, you are disobeying me. You are disobeying my instruction, then you will have to fight with me and if you are defeated, you have to marry Amba. Bhishma Dev said, okay, my dear guru Dev, if this is your command, I am ready to fight with you. And who is this guru Dev of Bhishma Dev? It is none other than the same Shakti Avesha incarnation of the Supreme Personality of Godhead, who is Parashurama.

---
### guru-disciple, battle, blessings
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zT9kEv50HVE?t=1356
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Because whenever a teacher sees his student surpassing him, excelling him in the branch of knowledge that he has taught him, the teacher naturally becomes pleased."
**Summary:** The lecture describes the mega battle between Parashurama and Bhishma, where Bhishma defeats his guru, leading to Parashurama's pleasure and blessings for Bhishma's vow of Brahmacharya.

You can imagine that powerful Parashurama is challenging Bhishma Dev for a battle. And Bhishma Dev, Bhishma Dev is also no less, he is a Mahajana, he is a great devotee of the Supreme Personality of Godhead and the both of them engaged in such a mega battle that even the celestial beings all came to watch this battle. It was such a huge, tremendous battle between the two great personalities. And eventually what was the outcome of the battle? The outcome of the battle was Parashurama, the guru was defeated by his disciple Bhishma Dev. And when this happened, Parashurama became pleased. Why so? Because whenever a teacher sees his student surpassing him, excelling him in the branch of knowledge that he has taught him, the teacher naturally becomes pleased. And Parashurama saw that my student Bhishma Dev, he has excelled me in the Dhanur Vidya which I taught him. He was very pleased and he blessed Bhishma Dev, saying that my dear Bhishma Dev, may your vow to remain a Brahmachari be successful and you will be able to fulfill your vow of Brahmacharya.

---
### desire, anger, revenge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zT9kEv50HVE?t=1434
**Verse References:** BG 2.62
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Kamad bhavati krodha, krodha bhavati sammohaha."
**Summary:** The lecture explains Amba's frustration and anger at not being able to marry Bhishma, leading her to perform austerities and take birth as Shikhandi to seek revenge.

Now Amba was left in the lurch. What will she do? She saw that her desire would never be fulfilled. As Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita, what happens when somebody's desire is not fulfilled? Kamad bhavati krodha, krodha bhavati sammohaha. So when a person's desire doesn't get fulfilled, one becomes frustrated. And eventually that frustration turns into anger. In Amba's case also, the same thing happened. When her desire to marry Bhishma Dev was not fulfilled, she became wrathful. And she wanted to wreak her vengeance on Bhishma Dev. But she knew that in her present condition, it was not possible for her to fight with Bhishma Dev in any way. So she decided to perform a lot of austerities and take another birth, where she would be powerful enough to kill Bhishma Dev. And that is how she did various very strict austerities she performed, very severe penances she performed, and she was born as Shikhandi, the son of Drupada, born to kill Bhishma Dev. So this is the story of Amba.

---
### succession, duty, incarnations
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zT9kEv50HVE?t=1505
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Therefore, Vyasadeva, although he was an ascetic, engaged in various penances and activities of spiritual life, he decided to obey the command of his mother."
**Summary:** The lecture concludes with Satyavati's dilemma and her decision to call upon Vyasadeva to beget children in Ambika and Ambalika, followed by a question to the audience about the type of incarnation Krishna is.

Now in the meantime, Satyavati, now that Vichitravirya had died and he had left Ambika and Ambalika without children, she thought, let me use the claws of Devarayana Sutopatthim and beget children in Ambika and Ambalika through Vichitravirya's step-brother. Now Bhishma Dev cannot fulfill that principle because he is bound by the vow of Brahmacharya. So therefore, Satyavati remembered her son from Parasharamuni before her marriage to Shantanu. And that son was none other than Vyasadeva. She sent word for Vyasadeva to come. And when Vyasadeva came, she explained the whole situation and understanding that this was an emergency, this was something which was the need of the hour to protect the citizens of the kingdom, ensure that there would be proper governance in the kingdom and there would be proper law and order within the kingdom. Therefore, Vyasadeva, although he was an ascetic, engaged in various penances and activities of spiritual life, he decided to obey the command of his mother. And obeying the command of his mother Satyavati, he begot children in Ambika and Ambalika. Now who are the children? Who are born out of Ambika and Ambalika? We will see that in the next episode. Before we sign off for today, I would like to leave you with one more question. We spoke about the different kinds of incarnations. What kind of incarnation do you think Krishna is? Under which category of incarnations does he come? Do a little bit of research and come back and tell us next week. Until then, thank you very much. Hare Krishna Jagat Guru Shri La Pravupada Ki Jai Nitaayi Gaur Premanande Hari Hari Bol

## Mahabharata | Part 31 | The Rigged Game of Dice | His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

### invocation, prayer

### Mahabharata, game of dice, plot
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EmlcTpLJkh8?t=92
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Duryodhana, Shakuni, Karna, and Dhritarashtra, Dushyasana, all these people combined together to lay out a scheme, a plot, how to trap the Pandavas and take their kingdom by cheating."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes the previous episode, highlighting the beginning of the dice game and the conspiracy hatched by Duryodhana, Shakuni, and others to cheat the Pandavas out of their kingdom.

And welcome to today's episode of the Mahabharata. In the previous episode, we have seen the beginning of one of the key moments in the history of the world, in the history of the Mahabharata, a pivotal point in the whole narration of the Mahabharata epic, the game of die. We saw how Duryodhana and Shakuni, Duryodhana, Shakuni, Karna, and Dhritarashtra, Dushyasana, all these people combined together to lay out a scheme, a plot, how to trap the Pandavas and take their kingdom by cheating. Because the Pandavas were too powerful for direct confrontation. We also saw how Yudhishthiram had a great power over the Mahabharata. He was hard excited and eager to turn that game around. He almost stepped out when Dhritarashtra killed him. Udhishthira Maharaj was unwilling to participate in that game of dice, but he was bound by the instructions of his elder, Dhritarashtra. Even though Yudhishthira tried to wriggle out of the trap set by Duryodhana and Shakuni by refusing to play with Shakuni, Duryodhana, on the plea of playing himself made Shakuni catch the dice on his behalf.

### injustice, silence, helplessness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EmlcTpLJkh8?t=540
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Despite Vidura's so many good advice, he would never accept it. He always was under the impression that Vidura, he is, is partial towards the Pandavas and he does not think of my welfare. Although in reality Vidura was talking for his own good."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the outrage at the suggestion of staking Draupadi, Vidura's failed attempts to intervene, and Duryodhana's rejection of wise counsel, ultimately leading to Draupadi being put at stake and lost.

Daughter-in-law of the Kaurava household. How can her honor be put at stake? And now Vidura could not tolerate it. He spoke very heavily against Duryodhana and told Dhritarashtra, please stop this. Don't do this. Now, what happens is when Duryodhana heard this, Duryodhana could not broke it, whatever Vidura was saying, and he started chastising him very severely, reprimanding him very severely. Because it is said that, if advice is given to a foolish person for his own welfare, instead of shanta, instead of it making him peaceful, it will only anger him. An intelligent person, when he receives the advice of a foolish person, he will only anger him. When he receives good advice, he will take it very peacefully and he will dwell on it, he will contemplate on it and try to make use of that advice. But a foolish person will think, this advice is for my detrimental, it is detrimental to my interest. And when good advice is given, he will not accept it. Duryodhana was like that. Despite Vidura's so many good advice, he would never accept it. He always was under the impression that Vidura, he is, is partial towards the Pandavas and he does not think of my welfare. Although in reality Vidura was talking for his own good. Duryodhana very severely reprimanded Vidura and Dhritarashtra was silent. He did not take any action. So Vidura also felt silent because he also became helpless. And so now Draupadi was put at stake. And again the dice was rolled. And like all the previous times, again Yudhishthira Maharaj lost.

### humiliation, revenge, slavery
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EmlcTpLJkh8?t=669
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public

**Summary:** The assembly is in uproar after Draupadi is lost. Karna, remembering past insults, orders Draupadi to be brought to the servant's quarters, viewing her and the Pandavas as slaves.

Now everything was gone. Nothing was left. The whole assembly was completely in uproar. Nobody was happy with what was happening, except for three, four people. Dushyasana, Karna, Duryodhana, Shakuni, Dhritarashtra. These were the only people who were really happy. Nobody else was happy. Now Karna started remembering the insult that Draupadi had made upon him in the Swayambhara where in front of everyone she had said that Karna, because of being born in a Sudra family, he could not participate in the Swayambhara. So he immediately told Duryodhana, now Draupadi is a slave. The Pandavas have lost themselves. All the Yudhishthira and Bhima, Arjuna, Nakula, Sahadeva, all the Pandavas have lost themselves. Now Duryodhana, Karna, Duryodhana, Shakuni, Dhritarashtra, Karna, Arjuna, Nakula, Sahadeva, all the Pandavas have lost themselves. They are all our slaves. And even Draupadi has been lost in the game of dice. So bring her, why she should stay in the royal household. Please bring her and let us send her to the quarters, the servant's quarters. Let her stay with all the maidservants. So our servant was sent to bring Draupadi from her inner chambers.

**Summary:** Draupadi, unprepared and concerned about propriety, questions the fairness of her being staked, sending a messenger back to the assembly with a crucial inquiry about the order in which Yudhishthira lost himself and her.

Now Draupadi had not dressed herself yet. Generally when we we are at home, we will be wearing some casual dress, we will not be wearing our formal dress. So Draupadi was in her casual dress, she was not planning to come out, so she was not dressed formally. So she was not in a position to come to the assembly when called for and when she heard from the messenger that like this, in the game of dice, Yudhishthira Maharaj had lost all the brothers. He had lost himself and he had lost Draupadi also and now Draupadi was told to come to the assembly so that she could be sent to the servant's quarters. She sent the messenger back with a question asking whether she had been fairly lost in the bet, whether she had been fairly lost in the game of dice. Did Yudhishthira stake her and lose her and then he lost himself or he first staked himself and then he lost himself. He staked Draupadi later in which case the whole, his losing Draupadi becomes questionable.

### assault, disregard, shame

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public

**Summary:** Duryodhana orders Dushyasana to bring Draupadi. Dushyasana forcibly drags her to the assembly by her hair, disregarding her pleas and violating protocol.

Now when the messenger came back and asked this question, nobody had a proper answer. So Duryodhana told Dushyasana, what is all this, you know asking various people to bring her, you go and bring her. So Dushyasana forcibly entered into the women's quarters. Actually men are not supposed to enter the women's quarters. Enter into the women's section of the palace wherever they are staying. The men's and the women's quarters would be completely different. But he forcibly entered there, caught hold of Draupadi by her hair, by her tresses and he started dragging her to the assembly. Draupadi was, she was objecting, she was saying, I am not dressed properly, I can't come in this state. There are so many elderly people like Bhishma, Drona, Dhritarashtra, all such personalities, all such personalities. They are there. How can I come like this? But he would not listen. He just dragged her forcibly into the assembly and pulled her on the floor of the assembly. And when Bhishma saw this, when Drona saw this, when Vidura saw this, they were beside themselves with tears. Actually it was not an insult of Draupadi, it was an insult of the entire Kaurava dynasty.

### inaction, silence, degradation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EmlcTpLJkh8?t=902
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public

**Summary:** Dhritarashtra remains unwilling to act against Duryodhana, and Vidura's efforts are futile. The assembly is in turmoil as Draupadi is humiliated.

Duryodhana did not understand this. Dhritarashtra was not willing to listen. He was not willing to go against Duryodhana. Now who is going to stop them? Vidura is trying his best. Dhritarashtra would not listen to Vidura, nor would he listen to Bhishma, nor would he listen to Drona. He would not listen to anyone except Duryodhana. So, everybody, nobody knew what to do. Everybody was beside themselves and the assembly, big furor in the assembly. How can Draupadi be brought here? So many people were objecting. Some people were speaking in favor of Duryodhana. Some people were objecting. And when she was brought like this, then Duryodhana started telling her, now your husbands have lost you. Now you are a slave. You please go to the servants quarters and be with the maid servants. And then Karna told her, told Draupadi, Draupadi, now all your husbands, they have all lost you. They have all become our slaves. Now you pick up somebody else as your husband. These people are no more your slaves. And these people are no more your husbands. Like this, such piercing words were being used. Draupadi was weeping and she was inconsolable. And she looked at Drona, she looked at Bhishma, Vidura, none of them are saying anything. Then she started talking to Dhritarashtra, oh my king, what is this? The honor of the entire Kaurava dynasty is being brought to the, in front of all the public like this? Is this the way to treat Dhritarashtra? Is this the way to treat the women folk of the Kaurava dynasty?

### dharma, confusion, helplessness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EmlcTpLJkh8?t=991
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Bhishma said, my dear daughter, I am unable to answer you because although Yudhishthira lost himself first and in one sense what you are saying is right, at the same time, no matter in what situation a man is, his wife is still his own."
**Summary:** Draupadi questions Dhritarashtra and Bhishma about the legality of her being staked after Yudhishthira lost himself. Bhishma admits his confusion and inability to provide a clear answer.

Dhritarashtra was silent. Then she asked Dhritarashtra, please tell me, if Yudhishthira Maharaj had lost himself before staking me, then how is it right for him to stake me further and then lose me? Because he has already lost himself. He is no more in a position to stake anybody else. Now she asked Bhishma. Bhishma said, my dear daughter, I am unable to answer you because although Yudhishthira lost himself first and in one sense what you are saying is right, at the same time, no matter in what situation a man is, his wife is still his own. Just because Yudhishthira lost himself in the bed doesn't mean that he lost you also. Even after he became the slave of the Kauravas, of Duryodhana and others, he is still your husband. And so he had the right to stake you. So this is also there. At the same time, this logic is also there that Yudhishthira, having staked himself, had no more right to continue playing. So I am confused. I am not able to give a proper answer.

And so now there was nobody was able to say anything. At that time. At that time, one of Duryodhana's brothers, Vikarna, he stood up and he objected. He said, Draupadi, her honor is being put on the floor like this. I am not for this. He objected very strongly. And he said, according to me, Yudhishthira Maharaj, the one, the moment he had lost himself in the game of dice, the game of dice was over. There was no more question of betting and there was no more question of further staking Draupadi. So Draupadi is not lost. She cannot be brought into the ashram. She cannot be made a maid servant. I oppose this. Vikarna spoke very strongly, although he was a younger brother of Duryodhana.

**Summary:** Dushyasana begins to disrobe Draupadi. Bhima is consumed by fury but is restrained by Yudhishthira's adherence to dharma and their status as slaves.

He was also a wild man. Immediately, he was also waiting for an opportunity. Immediately, he caught hold of Draupadi's sari and started pulling at it. Now Bhima. He was waiting. He was beside himself with fury. He could not control himself. But then what to do? He was bound by his elder brother's words. And his elder brother, because he is so much fixed in virtuosity and dharma that he is not going to go against his word. He had already given his word. He had lost himself and his brothers. So he was not in a position to say anything against what Duryodhana and others were saying because they were now their slaves. So Yudhishthira Maharaj could not say anything. He could not do anything. And Bhima was bound by his words. And Arjuna started pacifying Bhima. Calm down. Calm down. Don't become too angry now. Now is not the right time. Our hands are tied. So like this, Arjuna was constantly pacifying Bhima. And so now, the brothers were not able to do anything. And in the whole assembly, Dushyasana started trying to pacify Bhima.

### divine intervention, surrender, protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EmlcTpLJkh8?t=1222
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Then she remembered one person who is the refuge of all people who are completely shelterless. That is the Supreme Personality of God and Lord Krishna."
**Summary:** Dushyasana attempts to strip Draupadi. Seeing her husbands unable to help and the assembly silent, Draupadi remembers Lord Krishna and begins to call out to him for help.

She started trying to strip Draupadi by pulling at her saree. And Draupadi, she is trying to protect herself. She is trying to protect her honor. She is holding on with great effort to her, with great strength to her saree. But this man, Dushyasana is such a well-built, powerful guy. And Draupadi is such a delicate woman. She is a delicate princess. What she will fight against this, this bulky, hefty man full of muscles and full of strength. So she tried her best to hold the saree but she was not able to. And she saw that her husbands are not in a position to help her. She saw that the entire assembly, nobody is speaking against this injustice. So what to do now? Then she remembered one person who is the refuge of all people who are completely shelterless. That is the Supreme Personality of God and Lord Krishna. So she started calling out to Krishna. Krishna! Krishna! She started calling out. But then this Dushyasana is not giving up. She is holding on. She is calling out to Krishna but nothing is happening. Then finally she realized, I am holding on to this saree with all my effort. But ultimately I cannot protect myself. In this assembly when such big, big people are there, my husbands are such great heroes, such great warriors, they are not able to protect me. What? I will protect myself. My only shelter is Lord Krishna. So she let go of her saree. She raised her hands and started calling out, O Govinda! O Gopijanavallada! O Hari Murari! Like that she started calling out to Krishna.

---
### divine grace, surrender, miracle
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EmlcTpLJkh8?t=1328
**Verse References:** BG 15.15
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The moment she gave up everything else, her dependence on her husbands, her dependence on her own abilities, she gave up everything and totally surrendered to Krishna. Krishna as Paramatma immediately sensed that surrender"
**Summary:** Draupadi, realizing her helplessness, surrenders completely to Krishna. Krishna, as Paramatma, responds to her surrender and miraculously protects her honor by providing an endless supply of fabric, exhausting Dushyasana.

The moment she started calling out to Krishna, Krishna, although you are so far away in Dwaraka, but then Krishna, as he says in the Bhagavad Gita, Sarvasya Chaham Hrithi Sannivishtaha. Krishna is seated in every living entity's heart as Paramatma. So the moment she gave up everything else, her dependence on her husbands, her dependence on her own abilities, she gave up everything and totally surrendered to Krishna. Krishna as Paramatma immediately sensed that surrender and although Krishna was not there, Krishna was seated in Dwaraka. He knew everything of what was happening. And then he remembered his promise to Draupadi for the service that she had rendered to him. And Krishna decided now is the time to repay the debt to Draupadi. And so what happened? After that, Dushyasana pulled and pulled and pulled at her saree. The saree just kept coming and coming and the yards and the yards of cloth is being piled up on the floor. The cloth is piling on. Minute after minute, hour after hour he is pulling. Only the cloth is piling up but Draupadi is not getting disrobed. And pulling, pulling, pulling, finally what happened? Dushyasana lost all his strength and his stamina came to an end and he sat down on the floor perspiring in the midst of all the cloth. He was not able to understand what is happening, from where all this cloth is coming. And the whole assembly erupted in an uproar when they saw this wonderful miracle that was happening. Everybody understood how Krishna had saved the honour of Draupadi.

---
### alarm, injustice, plea
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EmlcTpLJkh8?t=1439
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "How can the Kaurava assembly be sullied by trying to outrage the modesty of a woman? That too not an ordinary woman. She is one of the women folk of our own dynasty."
**Summary:** Vidura and Bhishma, recognizing Krishna's intervention, become more alarmed and plead with Dhritarashtra to stop the injustices, emphasizing the dishonor brought upon the Kaurava dynasty.

So now the Vidura and Bhishma, they were all, they understood that somehow by Krishna's mercy this has happened and now Krishna is not going to keep quiet. So they became even more alarmed and they started advising Dhritarashtra at least now please stop all these injustices that's happening. How can the Kaurava assembly be sullied by trying to outrage the modesty of a woman? That too not an ordinary woman. She is one of the women folk of our own dynasty. How can this happen? How can you allow this to happen?

## Mahabharata | Part 32 | The aftermath of Game of Dice | His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

### Dharma, Duty, Injustice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=96
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So what use are such masters who do not protect the honour of their servants? So does that not free the Pandavas from their binding to their words?"
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the events following the game of dice, focusing on the moral obligations of the Pandavas and Kauravas, particularly regarding Draupadi's honor and the concept of duty.

Hare Krishna and welcome to today's episode of the Mahabharata. In the last episode, we have seen that very painful incident of the game of dice, where Draupadi was attempted to be stripped naked by the Kauravas, headed by Karna, Duryodhana, Dushyasana and others. And although Dushyasana tried his best to disrobe Draupadi, but then Draupadi surrendered herself completely to Lord Krishna, and Krishna in turn protected her modesty. And so, Dushyasana pulled and pulled at her Sari with great anticipation, but that anticipation turned to amazement when he saw an endless supply of her Sari covered cloth and that amazement turned into frustration that frustration became despair finally he sat down fatigued unable to disrobe Draupadi now when the incidents had gone this far Dhritarashtra had a change of heart what did Dhritarashtra do that is what we will be discussing today before we come to that we need to understand a little bit about the whole egregious happenings in that game of dice we all have understood how Yudhishtira was compelled to participate in the game of dice not because he was inclined to it but because it was the order of his superior Dhritarashtra whom he considered as good as his father now although Yudhishtira knew that this was a trap laid out to hurt him and his brothers Yudhishtira could not help it he was looking towards Dhritarashtra's intervention to correct the injustice that was happening during the game of dice but Dhritarashtra was silent all through now the question comes that when Draupadi was brought into that assembly and when her honor was being sullied by the Kauravas why did Yudhishtira and the other Pandava brothers not act was it not their duty to protect the honour of their wife? Yes, it was their duty to the extent that their powers authorized them to protect her.

---
### Duty, Kshatriya Dharma, Leadership
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=322
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Kshatriya is the person who protects his citizens from any kind of danger."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the duties of a Kshatriya, particularly the responsibility to protect citizens, and contrasts this with Duryodhana's failure to uphold his dharma as a king, while highlighting Dhritarashtra's violation of his duty to ensure justice for the Pandavas.

The fact is, that the Pandavas having lost all their kingdom and having been stripped of their Kshatriya position, having become servants of Duryodhana, no more were they, in a position, to be able to provide protection to others. Protecting others, protecting the citizens is the duty of the Kshatriya. Kshata trayate yati kshatriya. The Kshatriya is the person who protects his citizens from any kind of danger. It is the Kshatriya king's responsibility to give protection to all his citizens. Now that the Pandavas were no longer kings, they were mere servants, they were mere ordinary citizens, under the rule of Duryodhana. Now it was Duryodhana's duty as a king to protect the honor of the Pandavas, who are now also his citizens. Now if Duryodhana is not following his duty, if he is deviating, if he is flagrantly flouting the codes of Dharma, according to which he has to protect the honor of his citizens and prevent any kind of danger, then he is a king. Any harm to them, whether physically or economically or in terms of their honor, if Duryodhana, the king himself is flouting these duties, then what can the citizen do? So the Pandavas, in fact it was the duty of Duryodhana to protect Draupadi. He was not keeping up his duty. It was the duty of Dhritarashtra to ensure that there was no injustice to the Pandavas and even he violated his duty. Now because Duryodhana and Dhritarashtra and Karna and Dushyasana, all these people they flouted their dharma, their duty does not mean that the Pandavas also should forget their duty. Actually this was the biggest difference between Yudhishthira and the other Pandava brothers and Duryodhana.

---
### Dharma, Means vs. Ends, Virtue
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=475
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "On the one hand, Duryodhana was ready to violate every rule of dharma at the drop of a hat."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts Duryodhana's focus on achieving his desires by any means necessary with the Pandavas' commitment to upholding dharma even in the face of great adversity, emphasizing that the Pandavas were deprived of their authority to protect others when they lost their kingdom.

On the one hand, Duryodhana was ready to violate every rule of dharma at the drop of a hat. Why? Because his whole consideration was, his whole focus was the end. What I will get? What I want to get? How do I satisfy my desire? The means did not matter to him. He was ready to achieve his desire by hook or by crook. It did not matter to him. It did not matter to him that he was violating the codes of dharma. It did not matter to him that he was violating the rules of the Vedas. It did not matter to him that he was incurring grievous sin because of which he would have to suffer in the future. None of these things mattered to him. For him, all that mattered was, somehow I have to get Indraprastha in my hand. Somehow I have to insult the Pandavas. Somehow I have to insult Draupadi. This was his whole focus. Now if you look at the Pandavas, the Pandavas were reluctant to violate the codes of dharma. The codes of dharma. The codes of dharma. The codes of dharma. Words of virtuousness, even in the face of the greatest calamity. Even when Draupadi the wife's honor was put at stake, even there the Pandavas' consideration was, as per the codes of Dharma. What is our duty now? What are the steps that we can take? Or what are the things we should not be doing? So Uddistira Maharaj, from the Pandava's consideration. If we look at it from there. from that perspective, the duty was resting with Duryodhana and with Dhritarashtra to protect Draupadi's honor because she is also their citizen. They had been deprived of their authority to perform that function of protecting others the moment they were shown of their kingdom and they were converted into ordinary servants.

---
### Duty, Vedic Culture, Social Order
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=617
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even though a person may be very well capable of defending himself, still in Vedic times the people would consider, is it my duty to defend myself or is it somebody else's duty."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the example of Vishwamitra Muni seeking help from Dasaratha Maharaj to illustrate the Vedic concept of assigned duties within a social structure, where even capable individuals rely on others to fulfill their specific responsibilities.

We have examples of this in the Vedic history. Even though a person may be very well capable of defending himself, still in Vedic times the people would consider, is it my duty to defend myself or is it somebody else's duty. When Vishwamitra Muni was performing tapasya's and yajnas in the forest, there was a demoness who was coming and creating trouble for him, not allowing him to carry out his spiritual duty. Vishwamitra Muni is such a powerful sage. Not only that, before Vishwamitra Muni became the sage, he had been a king, a very powerful valiant king. So, it is not that he was unaware of the techniques of combat, how to fight, where to fight, with an asura, with a demoness. It was not unknown to him. Not only that, as a sage, he had powerful mantras by the dint of his austerities with which he could have easily killed the demoness. But he did not do so. He considered, my duty as a sage, what is my duty? My duty is to engage in spiritual activities. Whose duty is it? To ensure that my spiritual duty is fulfilled. That is his duty. His duties are carried on without any obstruction. It is the duty of the king. And therefore, Vishwamitra Muni went to Dasaratha Maharaj, who was ruling the kingdom at that time and he informed him, like this, there is this demoness who is creating trouble for me. So, you have to please solve my problem.

---
### Dharma, Duty, Varnasrama Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=836
**Verse References:** BG 3.35
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "There is a big difference between occupation for one's livelihood and one's duty."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the Vedic social structure with the modern world, emphasizing the difference between one's occupation (vritti) and one's duty (dharma), highlighting the various duties individuals have towards society, family, and even animals.

This is because during those times, in the Vedic tradition and the Sanatana Dharma, the duties of different persons in the social functioning of the society was very well laid out. In today's world, unfortunately, that societal structure has completely collapsed of the Varnasthama Dharma. Now we do not have an understanding of what is duty at all. We think we mistake our vritti, our occupation to be Dharma, to be Karma, which is not correct. There is a big difference between vritti and Dharma. There is a big difference between occupation for one's livelihood and one's duty. we have duties towards all other rest of the society. We have duties towards our parents, we have duties towards our forefathers, we have duties towards the sages, who have given us transcendental knowledge, we have duties towards devatas, who are supplying us all kinds of natural facilities, such as sunlight and air and water etc. For our livelihood. we have duties towards all other domestic, even to domestic, animals like the cow and the dog etc which render us service we have responsibilities we have duties we have obligations towards our other relatives it may be my husband it may be my wife it may be my sister my other relatives my friends whoever helps me in various activities i have obligations towards all of them there are duties prescribed in the vedas for all this so duty is completely different from occupation for one's livelihood one may give up one occupation and take on another occupation but one cannot give up one's duty and as lord krishna himself says in the bhagavad-gita one must not give up one's duty however imperfectly performed to take up the duty of some other person however perfectly they may be able to do it.

### Dharma, Duty, Virtue
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=959
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even though you are assigned the duty you are able to do imperfectly it is better than doing some other person's duty which is not assigned to you very perfectly this is the code of dharma"
**Summary:** The speaker reiterates the importance of adhering to one's assigned duty, even if performed imperfectly, over taking on another's duty, and contrasts the Pandavas' adherence to dharma with Duryodhana's focus solely on achieving his desired results.

This is against the laws of the scripture i may be able to do my duty say with 50 efficiency but i look at somebody else's assigned duty i can do that much better so let me do that no even though you are you are assigned the duty you are able to do imperfectly it is better than doing some other person's duty which is not assigned to you very perfectly this is the code of dharma and so the pandavas always even in that calamitous situation were considering what is virtuosity dictating to me what should i do and what should not i what should i not do is this my duty to protect draupadi or is it not it is the duty of actually the emperor the king dhritarashtra and duryodhana were responsible for her protection and if they are not performing their duty does not mean that i should violate my duty i should give up my duty this is a very big difference between duryodhana and dhritarashtra and the kauravas when compared with the pandavas duryodhana never had such consideration he never thought what should i do and what should i not do is my activity according to dharma or not he was simply focused on the result somehow i have to get the result the means don't matter the only the end matters whereas for the pandavas the end did not matter no matter how they were insulted whether they lost their kingdom whether they lost the honor of their wife whatever it was they would not deviate from the means the end was immaterial to them.

**Summary:** The speaker addresses the question of why Bhima and Arjuna followed Yudhishthira's lead, emphasizing the importance of respecting the eldest brother's guidance in matters of dharma and highlighting Draupadi's understanding and acceptance of the Pandavas' adherence to dharma.

Now the question comes okay yudhisthira gave his word now what was the necessity for dhima and arjuna to come to follow yudhisthira's this one he has given his word why could not dhima or arjuna jump in and then protect draupadi was following the instructions of their brother more important than protecting the honor of draupadi the fact is that draupadi is also the wife of yudhisthira so there is the responsibility equal now as far as the vedic culture was concerned the eldest person had the responsibility of guiding the younger people on the path of dharma the younger people always looked up to the elder people for guidance so everybody knew in that assembly that yudhisthira maharaj was dharma raja he would never deviate from the path of dharma his brothers also knew that and nobody knew the intricacies of dharma better than yudhisthira and so therefore the younger brothers dhima arjuna and others even though they could see such a difference between dharma and yudhisthira injustice being meted out in that assembly they held on to their firm faith in their brother yudhisthira maharaj that he knows the codes of dharma very well and he will tell us what is the real dharmic duty that they had to perform at that point of time so even dhima and arjuna were also very clear in their mind we have to do what is our ordained duty we cannot we cannot flout the the requirements of our duty we cannot violate the laws of dharma so therefore the bandavas are the most are the most pious people they are the most praiseworthy people in fact when the game of dice ended in this kind of fiasco even though the yudhisthira maharaj lost in that game of dice even though draupadi was about to be proved but lord krishna had to step in to save her the fact is even draupadi had no resentment in her heart towards yudhisthira maharaj she had full confidence that whatever yudhisthira maharaj has done was within the codes of dharma he could clearly understand that even dhima and arjuna were bound by the ropes of dharma of virtuosity and so therefore she had no resentment in her heart because she was also equally disposed to follow the course of Dharma rather than look at what was the result that had accrued out of that following of that Dharma. She had no resentment in her heart that she, Yudhisthira Maharaj and Arjuna and Jima, they followed the path of Dharma and they ditched me. There was no such resentment in Draupadi's heart. On the other hand, she was completely proud of her husband that even in that position, they followed the path of Dharma.

### Greed, Lust, Consequences
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=1260
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That was too much. Dhritarashtra also, Dhritarashtra realized that."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses Dhritarashtra's dual personality and his motivations, suggesting that he may not have intended for the situation to escalate to the point of attempting to disrobe Draupadi, while highlighting Duryodhana's greed, lust, and desire to insult the Pandavas.

So finally, Dhritarashtra, when he saw that things had gone too far, maybe Dhritarashtra's whole thing was, as we have seen, his dual personality. He had love for the Pandavas, he had love for his younger brother, as well as he had too much love for Gudiyodhana and the throne. And that love for Duryodhana and the throne overwhelmed his love for the Pandavas. So maybe Dhritarashtra had no intention to insult the Pandavas like that. His intention would have been to just get the kingdom and be happy with that. Although actually even Indraprastha was not his. Hastinapura itself, the throne of Hastinapura which he and Duryodhana were occupying, that did not belong to them. That was actually, you can look at it, because of the magnanimity of the Pandavas. They were able to occupy that throne. Otherwise, the Pandavas were the rightful heirs to that throne, not Duryodhana, not Dhritarashtra. But look at the greed of Duryodhana and Dhritarashtra when the Pandavas, from scratch, they built up their own kingdom. They wanted to go and occupy that. They were committing that. So Dhritarashtra's eye was on the opulence of Indraprastha. But even he didn't want to go and occupy that. He wanted to go and occupy that. He wanted to go and occupy that. He had not expected that it would go to the extent of trying to disrobe Draupadi. That was too much. Dhritarashtra also, Dhritarashtra realized that. But by that time, before he could step in, already the act had happened. Everything was happening very swiftly. You see, now we are looking at that whole event in hindsight. But when they were in that, in that whole assembly, when everything was going on, people sometimes have brain fakes. You know, that's a common terminology we use nowadays. Some people have brain fakes. Sometimes some people, even though they have full understanding and the knowledge to be able to act properly in a situation, sometimes people, out of some brain fade at that point of time, they do something which is contrary to what should be done. That was, similar thing could have happened with Dhritarashtra also. Because of which he did not step in at the right time. But when it had gone to this extent, and when he saw Bhima taking vow after terrible vow, that was the end. That was the end. That shook Dhritarashtra out of his slumber, weak slumber that he was in and he realized things had gone too far and he understood that this was something which he had not bargained for. Whereas, you see, in the case of Duryodhana, he wanted, he coveted their kingdom also and he also wanted to insult the Pandavas and he wanted to satisfy his lusty desire towards Draupadi. He got that opportunity and he tried to make, take full advantage of that. So, Dhritarashtra knew about Duryodhana's love for the throne, but he did not know about his lusty desires towards Draupadi. If he had known earlier, probably he would have taken steps to prevent this. But anyway, things went out of hand and things had gone too far.

### Conciliation, Consequences, Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFxsWxV9uTc?t=1451
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Dhritarashtra woke up and then he immediately put a stop to everything."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Dhritarashtra's attempt to rectify the situation by returning everything to the Pandavas and seeking forgiveness, while acknowledging the inevitable consequences of the events that transpired, and concludes with the Pandavas' departure and a cliffhanger about those remaining in the assembly hall.

Dhritarashtra woke up and then he immediately put a stop to everything. And what did Dhritarashtra do? He, in order to pacify the Pandavas, he returned everything that the Pandavas had lost. He returned the kingdom to Yudhishthira Maharaj and then he begged them to forgive Duryodhana and others for whatever had happened and he told them, let us forget and forgive. Although at heart, Dhritarashtra also knew that this is not something which the Pandavas are going to forget, nor is Bhima going to forget as well. This is, this is going to create lot of, this is going to create a big war in the future and all my sons are going to be in great peril. Dhritarashtra knew this, but still he was trying to use conciliatory word to try to, as far as possible to postpone the inevitable. That was Dhritarashtra's mode. And then Dhritarashtra, he said. He was a great man. He was a great man. in front of publicly in front of everyone he announced that Yudhishthira I must he actually he commended the Pandavas he commended Yudhishthira Maharaj for standing by virtuosity by standing by Dharma even in such great distress and having trying to and he used many conciliatory words and tried to console Draupadi also and then finally he he dissolved the assembly and asked everyone to go back to their respective places so the Pandavas accompanied by Draupadi with a very heavy heart they took their belongings mounted on their chariots and started back to Indraprastha their minds completely disturbed and their hearts heavy with the insult that they had faced with the dishonor that they had suffered from the Pandavas and they tried to console Draupadi with the insult that they had suffered from the Pandavas and they tried to console Draupadi however we have to understand that when a person gets insulted on account of some mistake he has done it is a cause for great pain but the Pandavas underwent such great dishonor not for any fault of theirs so there was no shame on their part the shame entirely rested on Duryodhana Karna Dushyasana and all the Dhritarastha and all these people so now when the assembly was dissolved they were all in a state of great pain and they were all in a state of great pain and they resolved everybody left to their respective residences and slowly the hall petered out and it became empty the Pandavas left for Indraprastha Bhishma went back to his quarters Vidura went back to his palace Dhritarastha went back to his room and the entire hall all the people who had assembled slowly they all left the assembly hall gradually the assembly hall became empty except for a few people who are these people and what were they waiting in that hall for join us in our next episode to know about this thank you for joining us today Hare Krishna Jagat Guru Srila Prabhupada Ki Jai Netai Gor Premanande Hari Hari Bol Jai Jai Jai Guru

### Mahabharata, game of dice, conflict
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PFyigGIKjcM?t=95
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "In the previous episode we have seen how after the game of dice, good sense prevailed over Dhritarashtra and he returned all those things which had been wrongfully, by way of cheating, had been taken away from the Pandavas by Shakuni and Duryodhana."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes the previous episode, where Dhritarashtra returned the Pandavas' possessions after the dice game, but the Pandavas are still troubled by the insults they endured.

In the previous episode we have seen how after the game of dice, good sense prevailed over Dhritarashtra and he returned all those things which had been wrongfully, by way of cheating, had been taken away from the Pandavas by Shakuni and Duryodhana. After the game of dice, after Dhritarashtra returned everything and he spoke a few conciliatory words to Draupadi and to the Pandavas, he dissolved the assembly and everyone left the assembly hall. After everyone left the assembly hall, a few people remained back and they were looking at each other and who are these people and what were their thoughts. Stay with us today to learn about that. The Pandavas after leaving the assembly, they stepped out of the place and they got onto their chariots and they left Hastinapura. They drove back to Indraprastha. Their hearts were heavy. Their thoughts were filled with all those words of insults and the attempt to disrobe Draupadi. The Pandavas were still fresh in their hearts and the wounds were still festering. The Pandavas, although at that moment, at that point of time, there was no way they could vent out their anger and frustration, carrying these heavy thoughts in their mind, they were going back to Indraprastha. After they had gone out of Hastinapura and they had travelled some distance, they were told that somebody was coming on a horseback trying to catch up with them. Soon the person who was on horseback caught up with the Pandavas and they saw that this person on horseback was one of the servants from the Hastinapura palace. This servant, he came with a message to Yudhishthira Maharaj and to the Pandavas. He said, Yudhishthira Maharaj. Yudhishthira Maharaj. Yudhishthira Maharaj.

### Mahabharata, Duryodhana, anger
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PFyigGIKjcM?t=232
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story

**Summary:** The Pandavas are surprised to receive a message from Dhritarashtra. The scene shifts to Duryodhana, Karna, Shakuni, and Dushasana, who are shocked and angry that Dhritarashtra reversed their victory. Dushasana expresses his frustration with Dhritarashtra's decision in multiple languages.

The Pandavas were surprised. They, after all that had happened, they just, just left the assembly and they had set out back to Indraprastha. But suddenly this messenger turned up and this messenger said that he was carrying a message for the Pandavas from Dhritarashtra. What was this message that Dhritarashtra had chosen to send to the Pandavas? Actually what had happened? After the game of dice and the Pandavas were returned their kingdom and whatever they had lost at the stakes and that they had left the hall and all the others who are who are present in the assembly had all left. Dhritarashtra had left, Bhishma had left, Drona had left and all the other people whoever were present in that assembly they had all left. Now after everyone had left Duryodhana, Karna, Shakuni and Dushasana were left and they were looking at each other because for them it was a great shock for all of them they had almost won everything but at the last moment Dhritarashtra's decision had turned the tables so they were still in shock of what had happened. Finally Dushasana spoke up that old man has gone senile we had the Pandavas and their kingdom in our grip and he left it off just like that. Buddha satya gaya hai jo hamare haath mein aaya tha nikal gaya. Moose nivala nikal gaya kai ketna du vaai ketala hai. Anda kelavan yedukuthaan ipdi pannano. A kelavan ayalak endu iti. Wap affah diyauta. Just jangal kaiyila vannadana ellan na sipitu. Kai bantit baayi kukki barili la muduka aapet illa halaput lie. Mandit kai k bandit baayi qaratu upmunche muduka illa halpa puta. So these were the feelings of Dushasana.

**Key Quote:** "All along until now, he had been trying to avoid direct confrontation but to somehow through some cunning means, somehow cheat them of their kingdom and cheat them of their royal authority. These were his plans."
**Summary:** Duryodhana fears the Pandavas will seek war due to the insults they suffered. He has always avoided direct confrontation, preferring to cheat them. Shakuni proposes another dice game to regain what they lost, as they cannot afford a direct battle.

Duryodhana also felt alike but there was nothing they could do what to do. But if the matter had been just that, that the Pandavas had been returned their kingdom, it would not have been much of an issue. But the issue was that the Pandavas had been subjected to such great insults that they were not going to keep quiet. On some pretext or the other, Duryodhana was afraid they would take a war with the Kauravas. Then it would be very difficult because Duryodhana very well knew the prowess of the Pandavas. All along until now, he had been trying to avoid direct confrontation but to somehow through some cunning means, somehow cheat them of their kingdom and cheat them of their royal authority. These were his plans. So all along he had always avoided direct confrontation because Duryodhana knew that if he were to directly face the Pandavas in battle, he would not be able to succeed. Chakuni then spoke up and he told Duryodhana whatever happened, happened. Now you see, we cannot directly engage in a battle with the Pandavas. We cannot afford that. Somehow Hastinapura was not ours. We have got it in our hand. Now if we get into a battle with the Pandavas and lose Hastinapura also in our greed for Indraprastha, then we are left with, we are neither here nor there. So we have to think of something immediately.

### Mahabharata, Duryodhana, Dhritarashtra, persuasion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PFyigGIKjcM?t=477
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Then Chakuni said, I have got a plan by which we can still save the situation."
**Summary:** Chakuni suggests convincing the Pandavas to play another dice game. Duryodhana takes it upon himself to persuade Dhritarashtra, who is initially against it, fearing more conflict. Duryodhana argues that the Pandavas will inevitably attack, so a preemptive move is necessary.

Then Chakuni said, I have got a plan by which we can still save the situation. What is that? Chakuni said, somehow if we convince the Pandavas again for another game of dice, then somehow we can manage to win back whatever we want. Now neither Karna nor Duryodhana nor Dushyasana were in favor of a direct fight with the Pandavas because everybody knew they could not win. What Chakuni was proposing seemed to be the only solution. But then how to do it? How could the Pandavas again be brought back to another game of dice? Duryodhana took the task upon himself and went and met Dhritarashtra. He spoke to his father and he tried various ways to convince Dhritarashtra. Dhritarashtra was like, no, no, why? He was not in favor of it. He said, already one game of dice, so much bad blood has happened between the two of you. One more game of dice, there will be no stopping the, stopping a war between the two of you. So Dhritarashtra was not in favor. Finally Duryodhana told Dhritarashtra, my dear father, Dhritarashtra has gone out of control. Sooner or later the Pandavas will gather some, on some pretext or the other, they will gather an army and attack us. We have to preempt this. So the only preemptive fight that we can do is, you have to call them for another game of dice and we have to ensure that we win back the kingdom.

### Mahabharata, Duryodhana, Dhritarashtra, strategy, exile

**Summary:** Duryodhana continues to pressure Dhritarashtra, arguing that the Pandavas are like trampled snakes who will inevitably attack. He proposes another dice game to send them into exile, allowing Duryodhana to consolidate power and make it impossible for them to challenge him upon their return.

Now, if we don't do that, we have to call them for another game of dice. If we don't do that, then what, what are we doing? We are inviting trouble. Because it is, the Pandavas are now, are now like snakes who have been trampled upon. Once we trample upon a snake's head, after that you cannot think of just walking away silently. The snake will follow you and it will attack you and it will try to bite you. The only way is, you have to defend yourself. If you have, you have to take protective measures. Duryodhana, hence, told Dhritarashtra that the only way to take a preemptive action is to prevent a battle from happening, which can only be done if the Pandavas are somehow sent out away from Indraprastha and we have to send them in exile somewhere. And for that, the only way is to engage them again in another game of dice. Not only this, Duryodhana told Dhritarashtra, let, let us engage them in a game of dice and let's take this time. Duryodhana told Dhritarashtra that the only way is to engage them again in another game of dice and let's take this time. And for that, the only way is to engage them again in another game of dice and let's take this time. And if the Pandavas are in exile for a sufficient period of time, Duryodhana said, with all the wealth in Indraprastha and with all the kings, I will get all the kings on my side and then my power will become really strong and the Pandavas, whenever they come back, they will not be able to face us. They will not be able to withstand the power of my army.

**Summary:** Dhritarashtra finally agrees to another dice game. Gandhari, upon hearing the news, confronts Dhritarashtra, reminding him of Draupadi's suffering and questioning his judgment. She advises him to hand over the kingdom to the Pandavas and banish Duryodhana to prevent further conflict.

Dhritarashtra, although he was hesitant, finally, Duryodhana's persuasive words finally overcame his hesitancy. So finally, Dhritarashtra agreed. And Dhritarashtra summoned the servant and sent him, dispatched him to order Yudhishthira for another game of dice. When Dhritarashtra took this decision and this decision came to be that the message started spreading everywhere that Dhritarashtra has again called for one more game of dice, the news reached Gandhari's ears. She came running to her husband and she said, I heard that you have called for one more game of dice. I have heard what Draupadi had to go through in that assembly. It is unthinkable how Draupadi could have been subjected to such ignominy. And somehow you salvage the situation by returning everything. Now you have gone round the bend. You are again calling for another game of dice. Really, I mean, what do you think of yourself? Do you understand the consequences of your action? This is absolutely, what you are doing is absolutely wrong. On the other hand, Gandhari, the mother of Duryodhana, she advised Dhritarasva. On the other hand, please hand over the kingdom to the Pandavas and banish Duryodhana from the family. You send him away from Hastinapura. Don't keep him here. This was a mother's advice to Dhritarasva. Duryodhana's own mother, she could see the evilness in Duryodhana and the righteousness of the Pandavas and she was fully in favour of the Pandavas and she said better to reject Duryodhana and avoid any sort of ill-will and fighting within the family.

But Dhritarasva, what was Dhritarasva's response? He told Gandhari, you see Gandhari, whatever I have to do, I have done. Now, whatever happens. By the will of providence, let it happen. I cannot help it. And he walked off. Now, Gandhari, what could she do? She had tried her best. She went back. And finally, this messenger, despatched by Dhritarasva, had now reached the Pandavas and he was standing in front of Yudhishthira with the message from Dhritarasva. What was the message? The message was, the king is calling you, Yudhishthira Maharaja. The king is calling you, Yudhishthira Maharaja. For another game of dice. Yudhishthira looked at his brothers and he said, I am helpless. It is the duty of a Kshatriya never to say no to a challenge. Over and above this, Dhritarasva is in the position of my father. He is my elder. He is ordering I cannot disobey him. The other brothers in Draupadi, they also understood and fully well they empathized with Yudhishthira Maharaja's position. And they could understand that there was hardly anything else they could do in the face of following the rules of Dharma.

### dharma, duty, ethics, selflessness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PFyigGIKjcM?t=891
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "As Kshatriyas, they had higher principles to follow. So, since they were Kshatriyas, since they were rulers, those principles took precedence over their personal welfare."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that there are different levels of dharma, from self-preservation to the welfare of family, society, and the environment. The Pandavas, as Kshatriyas, prioritize their duty and honor over their personal well-being, leading Yudhishthira to accept Dhritarashtra's invitation.

Actually, what we have to understand is, there are rules of Dharma for protecting oneself. And then there are higher rules. There are secondary principles and there are higher principles. At a very individual level, if I am very selfish, I take care of myself. Now, I am not living alone in this society. I am living in the midst of so many relatives, friends, servants, so many people are serving me, so many animals. And there are so many demigods, devatas who are providing me so much facilities and conveniences in nature. So, I am living in the midst of all of these things. So, I have duty towards my own, taking care of my own welfare. Then, I also have to think about higher principles. Welfare of my family. Then, welfare of my friends and relatives. Welfare of the entire humankind. Welfare of other animals. Welfare of this earth. Welfare of this environment. We have responsibility towards all these things. At an individual level, if you see, the Pandavas knew that this game of dice is not going to augur well for them. It will bring about their own downfall. But then, as Kshatriyas, they had to rise above the principles of taking care of one's own welfare. As Kshatriyas, they had higher principles to follow. So, since they were Kshatriyas, since they were rulers, those principles took precedence over their personal welfare. So, Yudhisthira could not refuse Dhritarashtra's order and he came back for the second game of dice.
